analogy: Think of "Biotechnology and its Applications" through the chapter's own opening images: Biotechnology, as you would have learnt from the previous chapter, essentially deals with industrial scale production of biopharmaceuticals and biologicals using genetically modified microbes, fungi, plants and animals. The applications of biotechnology include therapeutics, diagnostics, genetically modified crops for agriculture, processed food, bioremediation, waste treatment,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 10.1 BIOTECHNOLOGICAL APPLICATIONS IN, AGRICULTURE, CHAPTER 10, BIOTECHNOLOGY AND ITS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Biotechnology and its Applications is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that biotechnology, as you would have learnt from the previous chapter, essentially deals with industrial scale production of biopharmaceuticals and biologicals using genetically modified microbes, fungi, plants and animals.

The text explains that the applications of biotechnology include therapeutics, diagnostics, genetically modified crops for agriculture, processed food, bioremediation, waste treatment, and energy production.

The text explains that three critical research areas of biotechnology are: (i) Providing the best catalyst in the form of improved organism usually a microbe or pure enzyme.

The text explains that (ii) Creating optimal conditions through engineering for a catalyst to act, and (iii) Downstream processing technologies to purify the protein/organic compound.

The text explains that let us now learn how human beings have used biotechnology to improve the quality of human life, especially in the field of food production and health.

The text explains that 10.1 BIOTECHNOLOGICAL APPLICATIONS IN AGRICULTURE Let us take a look at the three options that can be thought for increasing food production (i) agro-chemical based agriculture; CHAPTER 10 BIOTECHNOLOGY AND ITS APPLICATIONS 10.1 Biotechnological Applications in Agriculture 10.2 Biotechnological Applications in Medicine 10.3 Transgenic Animals 10.4 Ethical Issues

The text explains that 178 BIOLOGY (ii) organic agriculture; and (iii) genetically engineered crop-based agriculture.

The text explains that the Green Revolution succeeded in tripling the food supply but yet it was not enough to feed the growing human population.

The text explains that increased yields have partly been due to the use of improved crop varieties, but mainly due to the use of better management practices and use of agrochemicals (fertilisers and pesticides).

The text explains that however, for farmers in the developing world, agrochemicals are often too expensive, and further increases in yield with existing varieties are not possible using conventional breeding.

The text explains that as traditional breeding techniques failed to keep pace with demand and to provide sufficiently fast and efficient systems for crop improvement, another technology called tissue culture got developed.

The text explains that it was learnt by scientists, during 1950s, that whole plants could be regenerated from explants, i.e., any part of a plant taken out and grown in a test tube, under sterile conditions in special nutrient media.

The text explains that this capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency.

The text explains that you will learn how to accomplish this in higher classes.

The text explains that it is important to stress here that the nutrient medium must provide a carbon source such as sucrose and also inorganic salts, vitamins, amino acids and growth regulators like auxins, cytokinins etc.

The text explains that by application of these methods it is possible to achieve propagation of a large number of plants in very short durations.

The text explains that try to visit a tissue culture laboratory with your teacher to better understand and appreciate the process.

The text explains that these hybrids are called somatic hybrids while the process

The text explains that (iv) increased efficiency of mineral usage by plants (this prevents early exhaustion of fertility of soil).

The text explains that in addition to these uses, GM has been used to create tailor-made plants to supply alternative resources to industries, in the form of starches, fuels and pharmaceuticals.

The text explains that a novel strategy was adopted to prevent this infestation which was based on the process of RNA interference (RNAi).

The text explains that the source of this complementary RNA could be from an infection by viruses having RNA genomes or mobile genetic elements (transposons) that replicate via an RNA intermediate.

The text explains that 181 BIOTECHNOLOGY AND ITS APPLICATIONS 10.2 BIOTECHNOLOGICAL APPLICATIONS IN MEDICINE The recombinant DNA technological processes have made immense impact in the area of healthcare by enabling mass production of safe and more effective therapeutic drugs.

The text explains that further, the recombinant therapeutics do not induce unwanted immunological responses as is common in case of similar products isolated from non-human sources.

The text explains that suddenly the whole process becomes so simple.

The text explains that insulin from an animal source, though caused some patients to develop allergy or other types of reactions to the foreign protein.

The text explains that 182 BIOLOGY In mammals, including humans, insulin is synthesised as a pro-hormone (like a pro-enzyme, the pro-hormone also needs to be processed before it becomes a fully mature and functional hormone) which contains an extra stretch called the C peptide.

The text explains that this enzyme is crucial for the immune system to function.

The text explains that can you explain how PCR can detect very low amounts of DNA?

The text explains that let us try and explore some of the common reasons: (i) Normal physiology and development: Transgenic animals can be specifically designed to allow the study of how genes are regulated, and how they affect the normal functions of the body and its development, e.g., study of complex factors involved in growth such as insulin-like growth factor.

The text explains that genetic modification of organisms can have unpredicatable results when such organisms are introduced into the ecosystem.

The text explains that therefore, the Indian Government has set up organisations such as GEAC (Genetic Engineering Approval Committee), which will make decisions regarding the validity of GM research and the safety of introducing GM-organisms for public services.

The text explains that the modification/usage of living organisms for public services (as food and medicine sources, for example) has also created problems with patents granted for the same.

The text explains that there is growing public anger that certain companies are being granted patents for products and technologies that make use of the genetic materials, plants and other biological resources that have long been identified, developed and used by farmers and indigenous people of a specific region/country.

The text explains that 185 BIOTECHNOLOGY AND ITS APPLICATIONS Rice is an important food grain, the presence of which goes back thousands of years in Asia’s agricultural history.

The text explains that in 1997, an American company got patent rights on Basmati rice through the US Patent and Trademark Office.

The text explains that several attempts have also been made to patent uses, products and processes based on Indian traditional herbal medicines, e.g., turmeric neem.

The text explains that biopiracy Biopiracy Biopiracy Biopiracy Biopiracy is the term used to refer to the use of bio-resources by multinational companies and other organisations without proper authorisation from the countries and people concerned without compensatory payment.

The text explains that in contrast the developing and the underdeveloped world is rich in biodiversity and traditional knowledge related to bio-resources.

The text explains that traditional knowledge related to bio-resources can be exploited to develop modern applications and can also be used to save time, effort and expenditure during their commercialisation.

The text explains that therefore, some nations are developing laws to prevent such unauthorised exploitation of their bio-resources and traditional knowledge.

The text explains that recombinant DNA technological processes have made immense impact in the area of healthcare by enabling mass production of safe and more effective therapeutics.

The text explains that since the recombinant therapeutics are identical to human proteins, they do not induce unwanted immunological responses and are free from risk of infection as was observed in case of similar products isolated from non-human sources.

The text explains that human insulin is made in bacteria yet its structure is absolutely identical to that of the natural molecule.

The text explains that the current interest in the manipulation of microbes, plants, and animals has raised serious ethical questions.

The text explains that it does so by replacing a defective mutant allele with a functional one or gene targeting which involves gene amplification.

The text explains that viruses that attack their hosts and introduce their genetic material into the host cell as part of their replication cycle are used as vectors to transfer healthy genes or more recently portions of genes.

The text explains that 187 BIOTECHNOLOGY AND ITS APPLICATIONS (b) toxin is immature; (c) toxin is inactive; (d) bacteria encloses toxin in a special sac.

The text explains that compare and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of production of genetically modified crops.

The text explains that how has man exploited this protein to his benefit?

The text explains that illustrate using the example of adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency.

The text explains that digrammatically represent the experimental steps in cloning and expressing an human gene (say the gene for growth hormone) into a bacterium like E. coli ?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10: Biotechnology and its Applications

cases.name: Bt Cotton
cases.point: Bt Cotton: Some strains of Bacillus thuringiensis produce proteins that kill certain insects such as lepidopterans (tobacco budworm, armyworm), coleopterans (beetles) and dipterans (flies, mosquitoes). (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch10 pg 2)

cases.name: BIOLOGY Specific Bt
cases.point: 180 BIOLOGY Specific Bt toxin genes were isolated from Bacillus thuringiensis and incorporated into the several crop plants such as cotton (Figure 10.1). (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch10 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 10.1 BIOTECHNOLOGICAL APPLICATIONS IN and AGRICULTURE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Biotechnology and its Applications; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Biotechnology and its Applications through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 10.1 BIOTECHNOLOGICAL APPLICATIONS IN and AGRICULTURE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
